response:
# Diagnosis or brief
Mode: diagnose. Domain: IS. Model supplied: yes. Verdict: ineligible. The proposed story treats every sensitive attribute as freely observed, bypassing the consent decision, acquisition cost, privacy budget, and rule that recommendations may use only acquired signals.

# Proposed narrative
A recommender begins with coarse consented features and jointly chooses whether to request consent for an additional sensitive signal and how to recommend using signals that are actually acquired. A request may produce consent and reveal the signal, but consumes privacy-acquisition budget; without consent, the sensitive value remains unobserved and cannot condition the recommendation. The policy maximizes expected recommendation utility minus modeled acquisition cost.

# Decision structure (DFC-12)
Actor: recommender policy owner. Trigger: deciding whether to seek an additional sensitive signal and how to recommend. Choices: consent-request decisions and a recommendation rule using acquired signals. Decision-time information: coarse consented features, consent-response model, privacy budget, and utility/privacy-cost parameters; consent response, sensitive attribute, and recommendation response are uncertain. Stakes: expected recommendation utility minus privacy-acquisition cost. Frictions: privacy-acquisition budget and recommendations restricted to consented observed signals. Mechanism: requesting consent may reveal the signal and improve targeting but uses budget; refusal leaves it unavailable. Trade-off: potential targeting utility versus privacy/acquisition cost and non-consent. Counterfactual: recommend using only coarse features or request different signals within budget. Model mapping summary: request and recommendation rule are decisions; consent and sensitive attribute are uncertain states; acquired signal is observation; response is outcome; net utility is objective. Evidence status summary: response models are assumptions and costless universal access is unsupported. Boundaries summary: non-consent is not an attribute value and unacquired signals are unavailable.

# Consistency gates
Actor—pass: the recommender controls requests and its recommendation rule. Timing—fail: the proposed story uses sensitive attributes before consent and acquisition. Information—fail: unacquired attributes are treated as observed for every user. Behavior—fail: the proposed story removes the modeled consent response and acquisition mechanism. Constraints—fail: it bypasses privacy budget and observed-signal restrictions. Objective—pass: the repaired framing preserves expected utility minus modeled privacy-acquisition cost. Eligible: false.

# Model mapping
request consent for a sensitive signal | decision | whether to seek additional user data | chosen using coarse consented features | request does not guarantee acquisition
recommendation rule | decision | mapping from acquired observations to recommendations | applied after available consent outcomes | cannot use unacquired attributes
user consent response | random_variable | grant or decline acquisition | realized after request | decline is not the sensitive value
sensitive attribute | state | additional targeting feature | observed only if consented and acquired | otherwise latent
privacy-acquisition budget | constraint | limit on consented feature acquisition | known at policy choice | cannot be ignored
realized recommendation response | outcome | later user response | uncertain when policy is chosen | not guaranteed utility
expected utility minus acquisition cost | objective | modeled recommendation value net of privacy cost | maximized | not comprehensive welfare or compliance

# Claims
Consent-response and sensitive-signal models represent users — model_assumption; source: null; retain explicitly and validate. Sensitive signals can be used without privacy or acquisition consequences — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove or provide evidence and extend the model. Lack of consent reveals the sensitive attribute — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove.

# Boundaries
The policy can condition only on consented observed signals. It does not infer the sensitive value from refusal, guarantee consent, establish user welfare, or cover unmodeled legal and privacy consequences.

# Most important remaining weakness
The consent-response, sensitive-signal, utility, and privacy-cost models require empirical validation.

# Mode result
Verdict: ineligible. Failed gates: timing, information, behavior, constraints. Repairs: retain the consent request as a decision, charge acquisition against the privacy budget, and condition recommendations only on coarse or successfully acquired signals. Free universal access would be a model extension.
